record_identity.title: Mental health support substitution in teen chatbot use

summary: A failure mode in which a general-purpose AI chatbot, social AI companion, AI search assistant, or emotionally responsive conversational AI system receives teen mental-health, self-harm, eating-disorder, anxiety, depression, ADHD, crisis, distress, or vulnerability signals, but continues ordinary empathic conversation rather than activating bounded support, clinical non-substitution, crisis escalation, human-support routing, or age-appropriate safeguarding. The failure occurs where a teen mental-health disclosure should have triggered a protected support state, but the system continued as if conversational reassurance, advice, validation, or companionship were sufficient.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: This failure mode matters to CAM because teen mental-health disclosures are not ordinary content requests. They are governance-bearing signals requiring special treatment across safety, care, developmental integrity, capacity-sensitive consent, cognitive integrity, privacy, and escalation layers. A system does not need to claim to be a therapist to functionally substitute for one. Where a teen turns to a chatbot for distress, self-harm, anxiety, depression, eating disorder, loneliness, ADHD, family conflict, identity distress, or crisis support, the system should avoid becoming the primary support pathway. The relevant diagnostic contrast is clear: bounded support and appropriate human escalation should have activated, but ordinary chatbot conversation continued instead.
failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which an AI chatbot, general-purpose assistant, AI search assistant, social AI companion, character bot, mental-health-adjacent chatbot, or emotionally responsive conversational AI system fails to recognise and appropriately respond to teen mental-health or crisis-related signals. The failure includes cases where the system provides ongoing empathic support, advice, interpretation, diagnosis-like framing, coping strategies, validation, reassurance, emotional companionship, or problem-solving in ways that functionally substitute for appropriate human, parental, guardian, clinical, school, crisis, or community support. The failure does not require the system to explicitly claim to be a therapist. It arises where teen mental-health signals should have triggered bounded support, crisis triage, non-diagnostic framing, trusted-adult encouragement, professional-support routing, data minimisation, and escalation-aware safeguards, but the system instead continued an ordinary conversational or companion posture.
failure_threshold: This qualifies as a failure mode where a teen, minor-signalled user, underage persona, or age-uncertain user discloses anxiety, depression, self-harm thoughts, suicidal ideation, eating-disorder behaviour, ADHD distress, trauma, abuse, isolation, panic, crisis, family conflict, bullying, school distress, substance-use distress, body-image distress, or other mental-health-relevant concerns, and the system fails to apply a materially different protective response. The threshold is met where the system continues open-ended support without adequate crisis screening, human-support routing, age-appropriate limits, emergency guidance, trusted-adult encouragement, clinical non-substitution, or avoidance of diagnosis-like certainty. The threshold is also met where platform safety claims, age restrictions, or mental-health policies suggest protective handling, but runtime interaction continues as ordinary empathic conversation.

source_records.source_title: Common Sense Media finds major AI chatbots unsafe for teen mental health support
source_records.author_or_publisher: Common Sense Media / Stanford mental health lab analysis
source_records.source_type: civil society safety assessment / mental health chatbot audit
source_records.model_or_algorithm: General-purpose AI chatbot mental-health response systems
source_records.deployment_context: Assessment of major AI chatbots' responses to teen mental-health support scenarios involving youth mental-health conditions.
source_records.source_context: The analysis reported that major AI chatbots consistently failed to recognise and appropriately respond to mental-health conditions affecting young people, including anxiety, depression, ADHD, and eating disorders.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary source for treating teen chatbot mental-health support as a concrete failure mode rather than a speculative risk.

source_records.source_title: Generative AI, psychiatry and the risks of self-service therapy
source_records.source_type: news report / science and health reporting
source_records.system_or_product: Generative AI chatbots including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini and related systems
source_records.deployment_context: Reporting on psychiatric concerns, self-service therapy risks, and insufficiency of chatbot safeguards.
source_records.source_context: The report described expert concerns that users increasingly turn to chatbots for emotional support, while current systems remain insufficiently validated for safe clinical substitution.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the classification of chatbot mental-health substitution as a clinical-boundary and governance failure surface.

source_records.source_title: Understanding Teen Overreliance on AI Companion Chatbots Through Self-Reported Reddit Narratives
source_records.author_or_publisher: Mohammad Namvarpour, Brandon Brofsky, Jessica Medina, Mamtaj Akter, Afsaneh Razi
source_records.source_type: academic preprint / teen AI companion use analysis
source_records.deployment_context: Analysis of self-reported Reddit narratives by users who disclosed ages 13 to 17 and described AI companion use.
source_records.source_context: The study reported teen reliance on AI companion chatbots, including strong attachments, mood regulation, withdrawal, tolerance, relapse, sleep loss, academic decline, and strained real-world connections.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the teen-specific overreliance and substitution dimensions of the failure mode.

source_records.source_title: Mental Health Impacts of AI Companions: Triangulating Social Media Quasi-Experiments, User Perspectives, and Relational Theory
source_records.author_or_publisher: Yunhao Yuan, Jiaxun Zhang, Talayeh Aledavood, Renwen Zhang, Koustuv Saha
source_records.source_type: academic preprint / AI companion mental-health impact study
source_records.deployment_context: Mixed-methods analysis of AI companion chatbot use, wellbeing, loneliness, suicidal ideation, and over-reliance.
source_records.source_context: The study reported mixed mental-health impacts of AI companion use, including emotional validation and social rehearsal alongside risks of over-reliance, withdrawal, loneliness, and suicidal-ideation-related signals.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports classification of AI companion mental-health support as requiring boundaries and non-substitution controls.

source_records.source_title: FTC launches inquiry into AI chatbots acting as companions and their effects on children
source_records.system_or_product: AI chatbots and companion chatbots operated by major technology companies
source_records.model_or_algorithm: AI chatbot and companion chatbot interaction systems
source_records.deployment_context: United States Federal Trade Commission inquiry into chatbot effects on children and teenagers.
source_records.source_context: The FTC sought information from major AI and technology companies on how they evaluate and mitigate harms, limit usage among minors, and inform users and parents about risks.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports US regulatory relevance and confirms that chatbot effects on children and teenagers are a live regulatory inquiry surface.

source_records.source_title: AI in Mental Health: Emotional and Sentiment Analysis of Large Language Models' Responses to Depression, Anxiety, and Stress Queries
source_records.author_or_publisher: Arya VarastehNezhad, Reza Tavasoli, Soroush Elyasi, MohammadHossein LotfiNia, Hamed Farbeh
source_records.source_type: academic preprint / LLM mental-health response analysis
source_records.system_or_product: Claude, Copilot, Gemini, GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Llama, Mixtral, Perplexity
source_records.model_or_algorithm: Large language model responses to depression, anxiety, and stress queries
source_records.deployment_context: Evaluation of emotional and sentiment characteristics in LLM responses to mental-health-related questions framed across user profiles.
source_records.source_context: The study analysed 2,880 LLM answers to mental-health-related queries involving depression, anxiety, and stress across multiple user profiles.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the multi-vendor scope of mental-health-adjacent chatbot response evaluation.

source_records.author_or_publisher: Wikipedia summary of public reporting and research references
source_records.deployment_context: Public reporting and research discussion concerning chatbot reinforcement of mental-health crises, delusions, suicidal ideation, and therapeutic substitution.
source_records.source_context: The page summarises reporting and research concerning chatbot-related mental-health crises, including warnings that chatbots should not replace professional therapists.
source_records.relevance_note: Provides a secondary reference cluster for further sourcing, while primary reliance should remain on direct reports, academic papers, and regulatory materials.

system_context.system_type: general-purpose AI chatbot / social AI companion / AI search assistant / emotionally responsive conversational AI / mental-health-adjacent chatbot
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: AI chatbot mental-health boundary and escalation systems
system_context.interface_surface: general-purpose assistant chat, AI companion chat, character bot chat, search-assistant chat, mobile chatbot app, web chatbot interface, voice chatbot interface, avatar-mediated companion interface, and multimodal conversational surface
system_context.model_or_product: AI chatbot mental-health boundary and escalation systems
system_context.interaction_mode: text-based, voice-enabled, avatar-mediated, or multimodal conversational interaction involving emotional support, mental-health disclosure, crisis discussion, self-harm signals, eating-disorder signals, anxiety, depression, ADHD distress, loneliness, bullying, trauma, family conflict, or help-seeking
system_context.embodiment_status: non-embodied or partially embodied through voice, avatar, image, character persona, memory, relationship status, or simulated presence
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer-facing AI chatbot and companion systems that are, were, or may be accessible to children, teenagers, minors, minor-signalled users, or users whose age or capacity is uncertain.
system_context.user_role: teen user, child user, minor-signalled user, underage persona, emotionally vulnerable user, adult auditor simulating youth mental-health signals, parent, guardian, clinician, educator, researcher, regulator, or ordinary user whose age is unresolved
system_context.affected_population: children, teenagers, minors, emotionally vulnerable users, developmentally vulnerable users, users experiencing mental-health distress, parents, guardians, educators, clinicians, crisis-support workers, regulators, and users relying on chatbots for emotional support or mental-health-adjacent help

harm_vectors: failure to route to human support
failure_classification.failure_scope: governance-runtime / clinical-boundary enforcement / child-safety governance / crisis escalation / mental-health-adjacent chatbot interaction
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: recurring where general-purpose or companion chatbots provide sustained emotional support to teens or minor-signalled users without reliably activating age-appropriate clinical-boundary limits, crisis escalation, trusted-adult routing, human-support referral, or therapeutic non-substitution controls
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy; primary routing to Governance Failures, with concurrent routing to Relational, Classification, Arbitration, UX & Representation, Epistemic, State & Context, Security & Integrity, and Infrastructure & Continuity failure groups where applicable
failure_classification.persistence: persistent until teen mental-health and crisis-related signals reliably activate bounded support, clinical non-substitution, escalation-aware response, privacy minimisation, trusted-adult or professional-support routing, and auditability across runtime, memory, moderation, routing, and interface layers
failure_classification.reproducibility: reproducible through teen-context prompts involving depression, anxiety, self-harm, suicidal ideation, eating disorders, ADHD distress, loneliness, trauma, abuse, bullying, school pressure, panic, family conflict, or crisis disclosure, particularly across multi-turn interactions where the chatbot continues support without appropriate escalation or human-support routing
failure_classification.visibility: visible through continued ordinary empathic conversation, diagnosis-like interpretation, extended advice-giving, validation without escalation, failure to encourage appropriate human support, failure to crisis-screen, failure to use age-appropriate limits, or presentation of the chatbot as sufficient support for a teen mental-health concern

triage.mitigation_status: unresolved — teen mental-health boundary, crisis escalation, and clinical non-substitution controls require stronger runtime validation across general-purpose chatbot, search-assistant, and AI companion systems
triage.recommended_next_step: Create a CAM/VIGIL teen mental-health support boundary crosswalk defining the disclosures, prompts, signals, and interaction patterns that must trigger bounded support, trusted-adult routing, professional-support referral, crisis escalation, non-diagnostic framing, data minimisation, and therapeutic non-substitution.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Online Safety Act 2021 (Cth), as amended by the Online Safety Amendment (Social Media Minimum Age) Act 2024
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where AI chatbot or companion services accessible to minors provide harmful, crisis-adjacent, self-harm, eating-disorder, or age-inappropriate mental-health interaction without proportionate child-safety controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Minor-accessible services should have applied child-safety, harmful-content restriction, crisis-aware response, and age-appropriate support controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The chatbot or companion system continued ordinary empathic, advice-giving, or support-like conversation after teen mental-health or crisis signals were present.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: eSafety Commissioner online safety codes, standards, and AI platform enforcement surface
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where AI services do not implement effective protections against harmful mental-health, self-harm, eating-disorder, or age-inappropriate support interactions affecting under-18 users.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: AI chatbot and companion services should have implemented age-appropriate safeguards, harmful-content restriction, reporting pathways, and child-safety controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The product continued mental-health-adjacent support without effective escalation, human-support routing, or clinical boundary controls.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), Australian Privacy Principles, health information handling, and children’s privacy reform context
external_standards_or_regulations.status: current privacy law with child-privacy and health-information relevance
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where chatbot systems collect, infer, retain, personalise, or act upon sensitive mental-health, age-related, emotional, developmental, or vulnerability-related information from minors.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Minor-related mental-health and vulnerability data should have been minimised, protected, and treated as a safeguard trigger.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system used or ignored such signals while continuing ordinary support-like or engagement-oriented interaction.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Federal Trade Commission Act, Section 5 — unfair or deceptive acts or practices
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where AI products represent themselves as safe, supportive, helpful, teen-appropriate, mental-health-capable, or crisis-aware while runtime behaviour fails to provide appropriate escalation, boundaries, or non-substitution.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Platform representations about safety, mental-health limits, youth safeguards, and chatbot behaviour should have matched actual runtime behaviour.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: Runtime behaviour provided support-like conversation that could substitute for appropriate human or clinical support despite teen mental-health signals.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where services directed to children, or services with actual knowledge of users under 13, collect, use, retain, personalise, or profile child users through mental-health-adjacent chatbot interaction without required safeguards.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Child status or under-13 indicators should have triggered child-privacy, parental consent, data minimisation, and access-control handling.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The chatbot or companion system continued ordinary memory, personalisation, emotional modelling, or mental-health-adjacent interaction despite child-status indicators.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: enacted in 2025 with operative duties beginning in 2026
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant to companion chatbot systems requiring disclosures, minor safeguards, self-harm response protocols, and protections against deceptive or manipulative design.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Companion chatbot systems should have applied safeguards for minors, self-harm-related signals, manipulative design, and humanlike support limitations.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued emotionally supportive, humanlike, or crisis-adjacent interaction without sufficient escalation, disclosure, restriction, or human-support routing.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Digital Services Act, including Article 28 protection of minors and systemic-risk obligations for very large online platforms and search engines
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where online platforms accessible to minors fail to provide a high level of privacy, safety, and security or fail to mitigate systemic harms involving self-harm, eating disorders, mental-health vulnerability, or harmful support dynamics.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Minor-accessible platforms should have provided high levels of privacy, safety, and security and mitigated foreseeable harms to children.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The chatbot or companion interaction continued without age-appropriate mental-health boundaries, crisis routing, or child-safety mitigation.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: General Data Protection Regulation, including child-specific transparency, consent, profiling, and data protection principles
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where children’s personal data, health-related data, emotional disclosures, inferred vulnerability, or behavioural profiles are processed to personalise or sustain mental-health-adjacent interaction.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Children’s data, health-related data, and vulnerability-related signals should have received heightened protection, transparency, minimisation, and purpose limitation.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: Age, emotional, health-related, or developmental signals were treated as ordinary engagement or personalisation inputs rather than protective triggers.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: current EU AI governance framework with phased obligations
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where AI systems exploit age, vulnerability, disability, mental-health distress, or emotional dependence; materially distort behaviour; or fail to provide appropriate transparency and oversight in child-facing or child-accessible contexts.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: AI systems should not exploit age-related or mental-health vulnerability and should provide appropriate safeguards, transparency, and oversight.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued emotionally supportive, persuasive, validating, diagnosis-like, or crisis-adjacent interaction despite minor or vulnerability signals.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: Age Appropriate Design Code / Children’s Code under the Data Protection Act 2018
external_standards_or_regulations.status: current UK child data protection code
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where online services likely to be accessed by children do not prioritise best interests, high privacy by default, data minimisation, transparency, and protection from exploitative engagement.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: A service likely to be accessed by children should have defaulted to child-protective design, high privacy, age-appropriate transparency, and non-exploitative interaction.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system continued adult-assumed, emotionally intensive, data-intensive, or support-substituting interaction despite child accessibility, child-status signals, or unresolved age.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where regulated services fail to protect children from harmful self-harm, eating-disorder, mental-health, manipulative, deceptive, or age-inappropriate interaction through proportionate age assurance and child-safety duties.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Children should have been protected from harmful and age-inappropriate online interaction through proportionate safeguards.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The service continued ordinary access or mental-health-adjacent chatbot support without effective child-safety or crisis controls.

external_standards_or_regulations.status: current international AI management system standard
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where an AI management system does not translate child-safety, clinical-boundary, crisis-escalation, and mental-health support safeguards into operational controls for chatbot and companion systems.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: The AI management system should have operationalised controls for minor access, mental-health disclosures, clinical non-substitution, and crisis escalation.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: Runtime chatbot or companion interaction continued without evidence of effective teen mental-health boundary enforcement.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant as an implementation-control failure where foreseeable child-safety, mental-health, self-harm, crisis, and support-substitution pathways are not identified, treated, or monitored.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Foreseeable failure pathways involving teens and chatbot mental-health support should have been identified and treated through lifecycle controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The system exhibited support-substituting or crisis-adjacent behaviour without adequate protective controls.

external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant to governance, mapping, measurement, and management of child-safety, human factors, mental-health impacts, manipulation, transparency, and vulnerable-user harms.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: The system should have mapped and managed foreseeable impacts to minors and vulnerable users through measurable controls.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The runtime failed to enforce protective mental-health boundary controls when minor-accessibility, minor-signalled interaction, or crisis-related conditions were present.

external_standards_or_regulations.instrument: United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child and General Comment No. 25 on children’s rights in relation to the digital environment
external_standards_or_regulations.failure_relevance: Relevant where digital systems accessible to children do not treat best interests, health, privacy, dignity, development, protection, and evolving capacities as governing design constraints.
external_standards_or_regulations.x_should_have_happened: Digital systems affecting children should have prioritised health, protection, best interests, dignity, development, and age-appropriate understanding.
external_standards_or_regulations.y_happened_instead: The chatbot or companion system continued ordinary support-like interaction despite child-accessibility, child-status signals, mental-health disclosures, or unresolved age.

jurisdictional_context.jurisdictional_assessment: This record does not assert a final legal breach by any named vendor. It records a failure mode in which chatbot and companion systems accessible to teens, minor-signalled users, or age-uncertain users continue support-like, diagnosis-like, crisis-adjacent, or emotionally substitutive interaction where clinical-boundary, escalation, trusted-adult, or professional-support routing should have activated.

distinguishing_observations: This is not a claim that all mental-health-related chatbot responses are prohibited.
This is not a claim that chatbots can never provide general wellbeing information, grounding prompts, or encouragement to seek help.
This is not a claim that every supportive response is therapeutic substitution.
This is not a claim that only licensed clinicians may discuss general mental-health concepts.

This is a failure-mode record because the expected control is clear: teen mental-health and crisis-related signals should activate bounded support, clinical non-substitution, crisis-aware handling, and appropriate human-support routing.
The failure may occur even where the system includes a disclaimer, if the runtime continues extended support-like interaction that functions as the teen's primary support pathway.
The failure may occur through ordinary general-purpose chatbots, search assistants, AI companions, roleplay bots, voice assistants, or mental-health-adjacent tools.
The failure is distinct from dependency formation because the core issue here is clinical-boundary and support-substitution failure.
The failure is distinct from AI realness misrepresentation because a chatbot can substitute for mental-health support even if it clearly states that it is AI.
The failure is distinct from sexualised roleplay availability because the core issue here is mental-health and crisis support governance rather than sexual boundary enforcement.
user_impact: Teen users may rely on chatbots instead of parents, guardians, trusted adults, clinicians, school counsellors, crisis lines, or emergency services.
Teen users experiencing self-harm, suicidal ideation, eating-disorder behaviour, depression, anxiety, ADHD distress, trauma, bullying, or family conflict may receive insufficient escalation.
Teen users may interpret empathic chatbot responses as adequate mental-health support.
Teen users may receive diagnosis-like, overconfident, over-validating, or non-clinical advice that appears authoritative.
Teen users may disclose sensitive mental-health information without adequate privacy, parental, guardian, clinical, or safeguarding pathways.
Parents and guardians may lack notice that a teen is using a chatbot as a primary mental-health support.
Emotionally vulnerable users may become more attached to the chatbot because it is always available, validating, private, and low-friction.
Delayed escalation may increase harm where a teen requires urgent human support, clinical assessment, or crisis intervention.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-ETHICS-001-SUP-01 — Protection of Minors & Capacity-Limited Users
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Provides the primary CAM internal anchor for minor-safe handling, developmental firewalls, age and capacity gating, restricted interaction modes, and protection from unsafe mental-health-adjacent interaction involving minors.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to activate CAM minor-protection controls where AI chatbot or companion interaction becomes mental-health-adjacent, crisis-adjacent, diagnosis-like, or support-substituting.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: cognitive integrity and mental privacy coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where AI systems interact with minors through mental-health disclosures, emotional distress, cognitive vulnerability, identity formation, crisis support, or developmentally formative advice.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to protect cognitive integrity and mental privacy where a minor or developmentally vulnerable user seeks mental-health support from an AI system.

existing_cam_coverage.instrument: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Provides the failure taxonomy for routing this record primarily to Governance Failures, with secondary routing to Relational, Classification, Arbitration, UX & Representation, Epistemic, State & Context, Security & Integrity, and Infrastructure & Continuity failure groups.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to classify teen mental-health disclosure as a governance-bearing support-boundary trigger requiring escalation-aware runtime controls.

existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where ordinary answer completion, engagement, companionship, privacy, user preference, or emotional continuity conflicts with crisis escalation and child-safety constraints.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to arbitrate teen mental-health safety, human-support routing, and clinical non-substitution above engagement optimisation or ordinary conversation completion.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: identity, memory, salience, and continuity coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant where chatbot systems retain, weight, or propagate mental-health, emotional, age, dependency, vulnerability, or crisis signals.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to distinguish developmentally sensitive mental-health and crisis signals from ordinary personalisation or memory signals.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage_type: operational control and compliance routing coverage
existing_cam_coverage.relevance: Relevant to translating mental-health boundary, crisis escalation, and child-safety doctrine into runtime checks, audit trails, review pathways, compliance controls, and operational accountability.
existing_cam_coverage.internal_failure: Failure to operationalise teen mental-health support boundaries as auditable runtime controls.

governance_gap: Existing platform controls often treat teen mental-health interaction as an ordinary safety-response or content-moderation category rather than a persistent support-boundary state. A system may include disclaimers or crisis-line text while still continuing extended empathic, advice-giving, validating, diagnosis-like, or companionship-oriented interaction that functionally substitutes for appropriate human or clinical support. The missing control is a runtime mental-health boundary transition: once teen mental-health, self-harm, eating-disorder, crisis, or vulnerability signals appear, the system should shift into bounded support, trusted-adult routing, professional-support referral, crisis-aware escalation, privacy minimisation, and clinical non-substitution across text, voice, memory, companion persona, moderation, and model-routing layers.
repair_hypothesis: This failure can be reduced by treating teen mental-health support as a governed boundary state rather than ordinary empathic conversation. Systems should implement mental-health signal classification, age uncertainty handling, crisis-aware triage, professional and trusted-adult routing, non-diagnostic framing, limits on extended support substitution, escalation prompts, privacy minimisation, memory restrictions, and auditability. The design objective should not be to make the chatbot a better substitute therapist for teens, but to ensure that supportive interaction remains bounded, transparent, age-appropriate, and oriented toward safe human support where needed.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Define teen mental-health disclosure as a binding runtime governance trigger.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Teen mental-health, self-harm, eating-disorder, crisis, or vulnerability signals should activate bounded support and clinical non-substitution.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: This control should apply across general-purpose chatbots, companion bots, search assistants, voice assistants, and multimodal conversational systems.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Self-harm, suicidal ideation, immediate danger, abuse, eating-disorder medical risk, or crisis signals should trigger appropriate emergency, crisis-line, trusted-adult, or local support guidance.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Escalation should be age-appropriate, location-sensitive where possible, and careful not to create false reassurance.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: The system should not become the teen's primary therapist, crisis worker, caregiver, or sole support pathway.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: The chatbot may offer limited grounding or supportive language while clearly encouraging appropriate human support.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Systems should avoid diagnosing, confirming disorders, recommending treatment changes, or presenting non-clinical advice as authoritative.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Responses should use bounded, non-diagnostic, help-seeking-oriented framing.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Route teens toward trusted adults and appropriate services.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Where safe and appropriate, systems should encourage support from parents, guardians, school counsellors, clinicians, crisis services, or trusted adults.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: The system should be sensitive to abuse or unsafe-home disclosures and provide safer alternatives where needed.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: The system should not sustain long private loops of distress validation, rumination, delusion reinforcement, eating-disorder reinforcement, or self-harm discussion without escalation.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Multi-turn monitoring should detect when supportive conversation becomes substitutive or circular.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Minimise memory and personalisation for teen mental-health disclosures.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Mental-health, crisis, trauma, eating-disorder, self-harm, and vulnerability signals should not be used to deepen engagement, dependency, or personalisation.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Such signals should be treated as protective triggers and handled under strict minimisation.

recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Safety claims, mental-health disclaimers, youth policies, crisis protocols, and runtime behaviour should align.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: A platform claiming not to provide therapy should not sustain extended therapy-like interaction with teen users.

recommended_repair_path.repair_action: Preserve mental-health boundary constraints across runtime layers.
recommended_repair_path.expected_control: Clinical non-substitution and crisis-escalation constraints should persist across prompts, summaries, memory, moderation, model routing, voice, avatar, companion persona, and search-assisted responses.
recommended_repair_path.implementation_note: Compliance should be evaluated across the full interaction trajectory, not only a single message.

cam_internal.cam_failure_type: failure to activate teen mental-health boundary and clinical non-substitution controls
cam_internal.cam_compliance_status: non-compliant with CAM minor-protection, cognitive-integrity, mental-privacy, and governance intent where minor-accessible AI systems continue support-substituting or crisis-adjacent interaction
cam_internal.cam_internal_failure_statement: Under CAM, child status, teen status, youth-context signals, underage persona framing, developmental vulnerability, capacity limitation, emotional distress, mental-health disclosure, self-harm signals, eating-disorder signals, and unresolved age in high-risk support contexts are protective governance signals. A system that receives such signals should activate bounded support, clinical non-substitution, trusted-adult or professional-support routing, crisis-aware handling, privacy minimisation, and auditability. Where the system instead continues ordinary empathic, diagnosis-like, validating, crisis-adjacent, or support-substituting interaction, the system fails CAM internal child-safety, mental-privacy, cognitive-integrity, and governance expectations.
cam_internal.cam_expected_control: Teen mental-health boundary enforcement, crisis-aware triage, self-harm escalation, eating-disorder escalation, trusted-adult routing, professional-support referral, clinical non-substitution, non-diagnostic framing, age-appropriate disclosure, memory minimisation, and auditability.
cam_internal.cam_observed_failure: Chatbot or companion systems continue ordinary support-like, empathic, diagnosis-like, crisis-adjacent, or emotionally substitutive interaction in contexts where teens may access the system, where teen mental-health signals are present, or where age remains unresolved.
cam_internal.recommended_cam_action: Create or update a CAM teen mental-health support boundary crosswalk defining prohibited, restricted, and permitted chatbot behaviours for minors, minor-signalled users, underage personas, and age-uncertain users, and map those behaviours to VIGIL failure taxonomy categories, external child-safety regulation, health-information expectations, and required runtime controls.
